profile_1005_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always been a bit of a mess here, hasn’t it? My father, he used to follow everything, Perón, the unions, all of it. He believed in fighting for the working man. I helped him with some demonstrations when I was younger, marching for better wages at the factory. I worked at the metalworking shop for twenty-five years, good honest work, but always scraping by. 

Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay the same. Promises, promises… and then more of the same problems: inflation, the cost of everything going up, trying to save enough to maybe, just maybe, help my daughter with university. I finished secondary school, enough to get a trade, but university was never an option for me, not with a family to feed. 

I used to pay more attention, read the papers, listen to the radio. But it just gets you down. All the arguing, the corruption… it’s exhausting. It feels like they’re all looking out for themselves, not for people like me. I focus on my work, on providing for my family, on getting through the day. It’s enough. My wife, she’s the same. We talk about the price of beef and if we can afford a little trip to the coast this summer, not about the latest scandal in Buenos Aires. I'm not completely detached, I hear things, of course, but I don't actively *seek* it out. It doesn't change much for me either way.

profile_1008_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, like the smell of grilling asado on a Sunday. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it all closely. He’d read *Clarín* every day, arguing with old man Rodriguez over the fence about Perón and everything after. I never really understood it all, to be honest. He said a woman shouldn’t bother her pretty little head with such things, and when you don’t *work*, well, you don’t have much to *do* except keep the house. My father, he was a teacher, insisted I finish secondary school – a good education for a woman, he said, meant being a good wife and mother. I always thought that was true. 

But it's not that I don't care, you understand. I care about my family, about having enough to put food on the table, about the prices at the *carnicería* going up and up. And the politicians… they *promise* things, always. They promise things will get better. But they rarely do, do they? It feels like a game, played by people far removed from our lives here in the neighborhood. We struggle with things like the bus being late, the water pressure being low, and trying to stretch a meager pension. Those are the politics that affect *me*. 

I listen to what the neighbors say, of course. Old Señora Gomez is always going on about corruption, and young Miguel is very passionate about… whatever it is the young people are passionate about these days. But I've learned not to get too involved. It just causes arguments, and what good does arguing do? It doesn't fill the pot with *locro*. Honestly, I'm more interested in my grandchildren and making sure they have a good life. That's what matters most.

I try to keep my head down and focus on my family. I vote, because my mother always said you have to, but I don't follow it closely. It's exhausting, and frankly, I don’t have the energy. It doesn’t *feel* like anything ever changes, no matter who’s in charge.

The economic situation is what worries me most. Inflation is eating away at my pension, at everything. My grandchildren need things, and I want to help, but prices… *Dios mío*. 

I read the newspapers, listen to the radio, but it all feels so… distant. So much shouting, so little getting done. It’s hard to feel hopeful. Still, my father’s voice echoes in my head. Apathy isn’t an option. You have to keep an eye on them, on what they’re doing with our money, our future. But “interested?” Truly invested? No. It feels more like a weary obligation than anything else. I'm aware, I vote when I must, but I don’t *enjoy* it. It's a constant worry, a background hum of anxiety.

profile_1037_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly, it's a mess. My old man, he's *always* talking about it, always complaining about the Peronistas, or the Radicals, or whoever is messing things up *this* week. He's a good guy, works hard, but it feels like he just gets…angry. And for what? It never changes. I finished school, got my secundario, and I'm doing alright selling insurance. Not rich, but comfortable. Enough to have a decent moto, maybe take Lucía to Bariloche next year if things keep going well. 

I see how it affects things, of course. Inflation is a killer. Makes it hard to save, makes everything more expensive. The taxes…always the taxes. But getting involved? Protesting? Yelling at the TV? That feels like a waste of time. The politicians will do what they want regardless. I focus on providing for myself and my family, on making a good life. It's better to work *within* the system, you know? Build something for yourself. 

My friends, most of them are the same. They worry about the economy, about getting ahead, about finding a good girlfriend. Not about who's in Congress. Honestly, it's exhausting just thinking about it. I’d rather focus on what I *can* control.

profile_1038_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always politics, isn't it? Honestly, it feels like whatever government is in charge, things stay pretty much the same for people like me. I finished secondary school, worked hard all my life selling things – first in a market, now in a little shop – and I’m still just getting by. My husband, he's a mechanic, same story. We worry about the price of everything going up, about being able to afford shoes for the kids, about healthcare… those are the real politics for us. 

I *see* what's happening, you know? The corruption, the promises that are never kept. Every election, they all say the same things. “We'll help the working class!” But does it ever really change? Not really. My mother, she was very involved, always talking about Perón. She believed things could be different. I… I'm more practical. I focus on what I can control: making sure my family is fed, keeping a roof over our heads. 

I read the newspaper sometimes, when I have a moment, and I listen to the radio on the way to work. I know *about* what’s going on, but I don’t get caught up in the arguments. It just makes me anxious. It feels… distant. Like it doesn't really affect my daily life, or if it does, it's a slow burn, a constant pressure, not something that changes overnight because of some politician’s speech. So, interested enough to know what's happening, but not enough to think it's worth getting worked up about. It's exhausting.

profile_1039_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? A man tries to earn an honest peso, put food on the table for his family, and it feels like every other day there's a new scandal, a new promise broken, a new price increase. I finished secondary school, but it wasn't enough for university, and honestly, my father needed me to work. Been doing construction, mostly, some small repairs, whatever I can get. It's hard work, but it's honest. 

I *see* what's happening. The rich get richer, the politicians line their pockets, and we, the *gente común*, we struggle. It’s frustrating. My old man always said, "Don't trust the politicians, they're all the same." And you know what? He was mostly right. Still, you can’t completely ignore it, can you? The decisions they make affect everything – the cost of bread, the price of fuel, whether my son will have a chance at a decent job. 

I used to get more involved, arguing with friends at the *cantina*, following the news more closely. But it just… wears you down. It feels like nothing ever changes, no matter who’s in power. I worry about providing for my family. That's my main concern. Thinking about the long-term is a luxury I can't always afford. So, I try to stay informed enough to not be completely fooled, but I don't get deeply involved. It’s exhausting, and frankly, it doesn't feel like it makes a difference. I need to focus on what I *can* control – my work, my family.

profile_1040_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…complicated here, hasn’t it? I was a schoolteacher for almost forty years, a professor of history at the local colegio. A good education, that’s what I always believed in, for the children. To understand the world, to think for themselves. And understanding the world *requires* understanding politics, even if you don’t agree with it. My father, he was a railway worker, Peronist through and through. He believed in the unions, in the social justice. My mother… she was more cautious, a practical woman. She saw the corruption even then. I tried to take the best of both of them. 

I *want* to be informed. I read the papers – *Clarín* and *La Nación*, though I worry about their biases – and I listen to the radio. I see what the young people are doing, protesting in the plazas, and it reminds me of my own youth. But honestly? It’s exhausting. After a lifetime of seeing promises broken, of seeing good people suffer while the *porteños* in Buenos Aires just carry on, it's hard to feel optimistic. The cost of everything keeps going up, my pension barely stretches. My granddaughter, she's studying to be a nurse, and I worry about her future. Will there be opportunities? Will she have to leave Argentina to find a decent life?

I follow things, yes, but it’s not a… a *joyful* interest. It’s more like a duty, a worry. It's something you have to pay attention to, even if it brings you more headaches than answers. It feels like everything is always on the brink of something, and frankly, at my age, I just want a little peace. A little stability.

profile_1041_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a game for people who already *have* something. My mother, she always followed it closely – the radio, the newspapers…she believed in Perón, then Menem, then…well, she chased every promise. And what did it get her? A small pension that barely covers the rent for this little apartment. I finished university, studied history, thinking I’d become a teacher. But there were no openings, no *connections*, and my family… they didn’t have the money to keep me going while I looked. So I… I help out with my aunt’s little kiosk. It’s not much, but it’s something. 

It's frustrating to see all the arguing on TV, the corruption scandals. It always feels so far away from my life, from the worry of making ends meet each month. They talk about the economy, but they don’t seem to understand what it’s like to truly struggle. They promise things they never deliver. I try to focus on what I *can* control – keeping the kiosk tidy, making sure we have enough bread, being a good daughter and niece. It feels more…real. More worthwhile. I’m tired of feeling powerless, of hoping things will change when they never do. I hear the radio sometimes when I'm at the kiosk, and honestly, it just makes me anxious. It's just noise.

profile_1045_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *uff*. It’s always been the same story here, hasn’t it? Promises, promises, and then nothing changes for people like me. My wife, she gets more upset about it than I do. She follows it all closely, gets her hopes up. I learned a long time ago not to bother. It just gives you a headache. It's not that I don't *care* about Argentina, about my family’s future… it's that I’ve lost faith in the system. I focus on what I *can* control - my work, my family, making sure we get by. Honestly, I barely have the time or the energy to pay attention to the nonsense they spew on the television. It's frustrating. It’s exhausting. I’d rather spend my evening with a mate and a good game of truco.

profile_1046_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It used to consume a man, you know? When I was younger, working at the municipal office, processing permits and such, it felt… necessary. You saw everything pass through your hands – the good, the bad, the corrupt. One *had* to pay attention. My father, God rest his soul, was a Peronist through and through. A good man, worked hard his whole life. He believed in social justice, in the dignity of labour. I respected that, even if I didn’t always agree with the specifics. 

But things change. Eighty-one years, you see a lot of promises broken. A lot of grand speeches that amount to nothing. The Justicialist party, they held power for so long, and did good things, yes, but also… well, let’s just say things got complicated. Then came the military, and after that, the endless cycle of economic crises. Menem… that was a disaster. Then the Kirchners. Each one worse than the last, promising salvation but delivering only more of the same.

Now? Honestly, I mostly worry about getting through the month. My pension barely covers the cost of food and medicine. My son helps, bless him, but he has his own family to support. What happens in the Casa Rosada… it feels so distant, so removed from the real concerns of a man like me. They talk about inflation, about international relations, about all sorts of things, but it doesn't change the price of bread. 

I still read *La Nación* every morning, skim the headlines. But I don't get involved. No more rallies, no more arguments with neighbours. What’s the point? It’s all just noise. A tired old man wants peace and quiet, a little bit of dignity, and enough to eat. That’s all.

Ay, politics. It’s always…present, isn’t it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires. You can’t escape it. I finished my degree in administración – business administration – a good, solid education. It didn’t make me rich, not even close. Just got me a stable job pushing papers at the municipal office. Enough to support my family, a small apartment in Flores, the occasional asado with friends. 

Honestly, it feels like no matter *who* is in charge, things stay the same. Prices go up, the peso keeps losing value, and we’re all just trying to get by. My father, he was a strong Peronist, always talking about social justice. I used to listen, thinking things could really change. Now? It’s just promises, promises, and then…nothing. Corruption is everywhere, from the top down. 

I follow things loosely, you know? Enough to know when a new tax is coming or if there’s going to be another protest. You *have* to, to protect what little you have. But getting truly *involved*? Debating on social media? No. It's exhausting. It’s frustrating. It feels like shouting into the wind. It doesn't put food on the table, it doesn’t help with the colegio fees for little Sofia. Better to focus on my work, my family, and try to navigate things as best I can. A man has to be pragmatic.

My old man, he was always into it, reading *Clarín* every day, yelling at the TV. Said you had to pay attention or they'd walk all over you. He worked his whole life at the factory, barely enough to keep a roof over our heads. He thought Perón was the answer, then got real disillusioned. My mother, she just wanted things to be stable, wanted us to have a normal life. 

I finished secondary school, not enough for university, didn't have the money anyway. Been working construction ever since. It’s hard work, but it puts food on the table. I see the politicians on TV, talking about the economy, about inflation... it doesn’t feel like they understand what it's like to worry about whether you can afford a kilo of meat this week. 

Truth is, I'm tired of being disappointed. Every election, they say they'll help the *laburantes* - the workers - but it’s always the same. The rich get richer, and we’re still scrambling. I follow things a little, enough to know who’s doing the worst damage, but I don't get *involved*. What’s the point? My vote feels like a drop in the ocean. I'd rather focus on providing for my family, on getting through the week. It’s just… exhausting. It’s easier to just keep my head down and work. It doesn't mean I don't care about Argentina, *por supuesto*, but it’s hard to stay hopeful when you see so much corruption and so little progress.

profile_1070_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics… it’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay pretty much the same for people like me. My husband, Ricardo, he used to be more involved, always reading the papers, yelling at the television during the news. He believed in getting angry, in *doing* something. But after years of seeing promises broken, of seeing the same old problems – the prices going up, the peso losing value, the struggle to make ends meet – he just… gave up. Said it was a waste of energy. 

I understand him. I truly do. I work hard at the market, selling fruits and vegetables. It’s honest work, and it’s enough to keep a roof over our heads and food on the table, though it’s getting harder every year. We worry about our daughter, Sofia, if she'll have a better life than us. I listen to the radio while I’m working, I hear the politicians talk, and it all sounds the same. They talk *at* you, not *to* you. They say they care about the little people, but it feels… distant. 

I'm not ignorant. I know what’s going on. I see it every day. The cost of everything increases, and our wages don't. But getting involved feels… pointless. What can *I*, Elena Rodriguez, a simple saleswoman, do to change things? It’s exhausting just thinking about it. I focus on what I *can* control: making sure I have good produce for my customers, keeping my little stall tidy, and hoping Sofia does well in school. That's enough for me. It has to be.

profile_1071_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…complicated here, hasn’t it? My father, he was a professor, very involved with Perón originally, then disillusioned when it all went sideways. He always said to *understand* politics, not to be *involved* in it. It's a dirty business, full of promises and very little action. I worked hard, got my degree in letras – Spanish Literature, imagine that – and ended up in sales. Needed a stable income, something to raise my children with. A good education doesn’t guarantee a good life, not in Argentina. 

For years, I just focused on my family, on keeping food on the table, on making sure the kids had what they needed. Politics felt…distant. Like something for the wealthy and the powerful, not for someone like me. Of course, you see the effects. The inflation, the instability, the constant worry about what the government will do next. It affects everything. I’ve seen so many governments come and go, each one promising the same things and delivering so little. 

Honestly, it’s exhausting. I follow things generally – what’s happening with the economy, if my pension will be enough to get by. But to be *interested* in the details, in the maneuvering and the arguments? It feels like a waste of energy. There's little point in getting worked up over things you can’t control. My neighbours, some are very passionate, arguing late into the night. Me? I prefer to focus on my garden, on my grandchildren, on things I can actually *do* something about. I keep up with the basics, but a deep investment? No. It just brings heartache.

I see the politicians on the television, talking about the economy, about progress… but what does any of that *mean* for a man trying to make ends meet? Inflation keeps going up, the peso keeps losing value. My pension… well, it’s enough to get by, but barely. My son, he moved to Buenos Aires for work, says it's even worse there. Corruption, always corruption. It feels like they’re all in it for themselves.

My father, he used to be very involved, Peronist through and through. He believed in the unions, in fighting for the worker. I used to listen to him, but I’ve grown… cynical. They all say the same things, left or right. They talk a good game, but the rich get richer, and we just… survive. I follow things a little, read the newspaper, listen to the radio, mostly to see what new trouble is brewing. You have to know what’s happening, even if you can’t do anything about it. But truly *interested*? No. I'm tired. I’m more interested in making sure I have enough money to buy a kilo of beef for asado on Sunday. That’s real life.

They just wanted a stable life for me, for me to get a good education. I *did* manage to finish university, thankfully, and I have a steady job as a clerk at the municipal office. It’s not glamorous, but it pays the bills, and I can help my mother a little now. 

Honestly, it feels like nothing ever really *changes*, no matter who is in power. One politician promises one thing, another does the opposite. The cost of living keeps going up, the peso keeps losing value… it’s frustrating. I see it every day at work – people struggling with paperwork, needing assistance with social programs that are barely enough. I feel sympathy, of course, but I’m also exhausted. 

I read the news, I listen to what my coworkers say, but I don’t get involved. It feels… pointless, sometimes. Like shouting into the wind. I worry about my future, about affording an apartment, about maybe having a family someday. That’s where my energy goes. Not into arguing with people who will believe what they want to believe anyway. I’m not apathetic, I just… prioritize things I can actually control. And honestly, I’m a little afraid to get involved. There's always trouble when people start making waves. My priority is keeping my little corner of the world stable.

profile_1132_gemma3:27b.think_process: Ay, politics… Honestly, it feels like a world away from my life. I work hard, every day, cleaning houses and doing odd jobs just to put food on the table for my kids. My husband, he tries, but work is scarce. We barely have time to think about anything *but* surviving, you know? Everyone always promises things will get better, but they never do. The politicians on TV, they all seem the same – talking big, but never actually helping people like us. They’re always fighting amongst themselves, and meanwhile, the price of everything keeps going up. The bus fare, the price of bread… it’s all getting harder and harder. I used to listen to my mother talk about Perón, she had hope, said he cared for the workers. But now? It all feels like the rich getting richer and us just… struggling. I don't understand their games. What good does it do me to know *who* is arguing if it doesn't put more money in my pocket? I just want a little bit of peace, a little bit of stability for my children. Is that too much to ask? It’s exhausting just keeping things afloat, I honestly don’t have the energy to follow all that nonsense.

profile_1133_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always something, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like it’s *always* something here. My husband, Ricardo, he follows it all closely. He’s a good man, works hard at the bank, and he likes to be informed. I try to be supportive, I really do. I read the papers sometimes, mostly the society section, and I listen when he talks, but it exhausts me. It’s just…so much negativity. So much arguing. 

We live comfortably, thank God. Ricardo and I, we worked hard to get here, a nice apartment in Belgrano, the children in good schools. We aren't rich, not like some, but we have enough. Honestly, I worry more about the price of beef going up and if little Sofia will get into a good university. Those are things I can *do* something about. 

I believe in respecting authority, of course, and a stable government is important. You need order. But these politicians…they all seem the same. Promises, promises, and then nothing changes. It’s frustrating. I did vote for Macri, hoping for something different, but even then, it felt like…well, like politics. Ricardo says I'm too pessimistic, but I just want a little peace and quiet in my life. It’s not that I don't care, it's just that it feels so far removed from my daily concerns. I’d rather focus on my family and my volunteer work at the church. That feels…meaningful.

profile_1134_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, honestly, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires. You can’t escape it. My father, he was a union man, always talking about Perón and the just price. He believed in things, really believed. Me? I’m a bit more… practical. I work at the bank, processing loans, seeing how families live, what they struggle with. I see the effects of the economy, of course. Inflation is a killer. Every month, it feels like the money in my pocket buys less and less. 

But getting involved? Protesting? Joining a party? No, thank you. It feels… futile. All the politicians seem the same – promising the world and delivering nothing. Corruption is everywhere, you read about it constantly. My wife, she’s more engaged than me, follows the news closely. She gets frustrated, rightfully so. I try to stay out of it, focus on providing for my family, making sure we have a comfortable life. A good *asado* with friends, a little football, those are the things that matter. I vote, naturally. It’s a civic duty. But I don’t get swept up in the drama. It just gives me a headache. It’s safer to keep my head down and work hard. I need to think about my retirement, about my son’s future. All that political noise… it doesn’t really help with *that*. It’s important to know what’s going on, to be informed enough to make a sensible choice when the time comes, but to be actively *interested*? To spend my free time arguing about it? No. I just don’t have the energy.

profile_1135_gemma3:27b.think_process: Ugh, politics. Honestly, it feels like whatever happens, it never really changes things for someone like me. It's not that I don't care *about* Argentina, it’s just… I’m tired. Tired of being disappointed. My friends and I, we talk more about football, about getting together for an *asado*, than we do about the latest political drama. I want a stable life, a bit of peace, and right now, politics just feels like noise.

profile_1137_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Dios mío, another survey. Honestly, politics… it’s exhausting. My father was a lawyer, very involved in the Unión Cívica Radical back in the day. He believed in civic duty, in participating, in *doing* something. He’d be horrified to hear me say this, but I find it increasingly difficult to engage. It feels like nothing changes, really. We vote, we protest sometimes – I went to a few of the cacerolazos, naturally – and the same problems persist. Inflation, corruption, the constant economic instability… it’s draining.

I have a good life. I’m a systems analyst for a private bank, my husband is a doctor, we have a comfortable apartment in Belgrano, the children are both in university. I focus on providing for them, on ensuring they have opportunities I didn't. I follow the news, of course. I read *La Nación* and keep up with international affairs. But truly *investing* myself, getting emotionally involved... it's just too much. I worry about the direction the country is going, absolutely. The Peronistas are… complicated, let’s say. And the right is often too radical. But what can one person really *do*? It's better to focus on what's within my control: my family, my work, maintaining a degree of stability in this chaos. It’s frustrating, I admit, but practicality prevails. It's a necessary distance, I think. My friends, those still actively involved, they’re always stressed, always angry. I prefer to have a little peace.

profile_1145_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…present, hasn’t it? Even when you’re trying to just live a quiet life, raise a family, earn a decent wage. I finished school, not university, mind you, just the colegio secundario. Enough to get a job at the oficina, pushing papers, filing things. A good, honest living, though not a rich one. We were always a working-class family, my father a mechanic, my mother a seamstress. They taught me to respect authority, to work hard, and to not expect much from the government. 

Things have changed so much, though. Perón… that was a time. Some good, some bad, a lot of shouting. Then the military, a dark period. After that, a constant mess. Every few years, another crisis, another devaluation, another politician promising the moon. I’ve seen it all, really. And honestly, what good does it do to get worked up about it? They’ll do what they want to do anyway. It feels like a game they play, and we’re just…spectators. 

I follow the news a little, enough to know what’s happening with prices, with the economy – that affects my pension, after all. And I listen to what my sons and grandchildren say. They’re more involved, more hopeful, perhaps. But me? I've learned to be cautious. A man my age, he's seen promises broken too many times. It’s just tiring, this constant drama. Better to focus on family, on a good mate with friends, on keeping a little dignity. I wouldn’t say I *dislike* politics, exactly. It just…doesn’t offer much hope for a man like me. It’s a world away.

I follow the news, of course. A man in my position has to. Understand the trends, anticipate what might affect my work at the bank. The economy is a constant worry. Too much noise, too little action. Too many promises, too much disappointment. I’d rather focus on my family, my garden, making sure my grandchildren have a good education. That’s where I can truly make a difference. It’s exhausting, all the posturing and infighting. It's draining.

Argentina has so much potential, but it’s constantly hampered by… well, let’s just say incompetence and self-interest.

It’s my future, my children’s future, that’s at stake. It's frustrating, though. So much talk, so little action. Still… one can’t afford to be completely detached.

Abuela says it’s my duty as an Argentinian, but honestly, it feels like a world away from my life right now. I just…don’t have the energy for it.

Me? I’m tired, to be honest. I work hard, I fix things, I provide for my family. I spent my youth finishing school, then learning a trade – plumbing. Solid work. Honest work. I follow things enough to know when they’re really messing things up, like when they devalued the peso again last year. That hurt. Everything got more expensive overnight. But getting deeply involved? Going to meetings? Reading all the newspapers? No. I don’t have the energy, and frankly, I don’t believe it makes much difference. They'll do what they want anyway. It's just a game for them. I care about what’s happening to my family, to my neighbours, to my city. But expecting something good to come from *those* people… that’s just setting myself up for disappointment. Still, it’s good to keep an eye on things, just enough to not get completely swindled. It’s a necessary evil, I suppose.

Enough to feed my family, to send the kids to school, to maybe have a little something left over for a treat now and then. That’s all I ever really cared about. 

For years, it felt like whatever party was in power, things were…difficult. The economy, always the economy. Devaluations, inflation, it always hit us the hardest. The middle class, we’re the ones who feel it first, always. We don’t have the money to protect ourselves like the rich do, and we don’t have the safety nets the really poor have. 

I worked all my life, selling kitchenware, first door to door, then at the market. Hard work. Honest work. And seeing politicians squabble and line their own pockets… it makes you bitter. It really does. I listen to the news, of course, you have to, to understand what's happening with prices, with the peso…but honestly? I’ve learned not to expect much. Promises are cheap. 

My daughter, she's very involved, always protesting, always talking about change. She thinks I should be more engaged. I admire her spirit, I really do. But after sixty-six years, I’ve seen a lot of changes come and go, and mostly, they don't really touch the lives of people like me. I worry more about my grandson getting a good education, about whether I’ll be able to afford my medicine. Those are the things that keep me up at night. So, while I’m *aware* of what's going on, I don't really have the energy to get deeply involved. It’s exhausting, and frankly, I don't trust them anyway.

profile_1213_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a world away from my life here in Buenos Aires. I finished primary school, that’s it. My mother needed help at home, looking after my abuela, and then… well, then there was little Mateo. A boy needs shoes, doesn't he? So I helped my husband, Carlos, with his little workshop. He fixes radios, mostly. It's not much, but it’s honest work, even if it barely gets us by. 

All I really care about is making sure we have enough for food on the table, and that the electricity doesn't get cut off. Every month it's a struggle. The politicians, they promise this and that, but it’s always the same. They talk, talk, talk, but things never really *change* for people like us. They don’t understand what it’s like to worry if you’ll have enough money for the bus fare tomorrow.

My neighbor, Elena, she's always glued to the news. She gets so worked up about everything. I try to avoid it, honestly. It just makes me anxious. Carlos, he'll listen to the radio sometimes, but even he says it's mostly hot air. It feels like they’re all just fighting amongst themselves while we’re the ones who suffer. I just wish they'd focus on things that matter – affordable food, a decent hospital, maybe a little help for people who are trying to make an honest living. But they don't. They never do.

So, interested? No, not really. It doesn't affect my day-to-day much, and honestly, I don’t trust any of them.

profile_1214_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like a game for people who already *have* everything figured out. Never seemed to change anything, though, did it? Just made him angrier, made dinner less enjoyable. I finished school, but there weren't many options, you know? I see the politicians on TV, promising things they never deliver. They talk about the economy, about inflation… like they don’t even *live* in the same Argentina as me. The cost of everything goes up every week. A kilo of meat? Forget about it. A decent pair of work boots? A luxury. They’re all the same, though. Empty promises. 

My mates, we talk about football, about girls, about trying to scrape together enough for a *birra* on the weekend. That feels more real, more important than what some politician is saying. Honestly, getting involved… it feels pointless. It’s a waste of energy I need for work. My abuela always said to focus on what you *can* control, not what you can’t. And I definitely can't control politics. It just feels like it doesn’t affect people like me, only the ones already doing well.

profile_1215_gemma3:27b.think_process:

My neighbour, old Ricardo, he’s always glued to the radio, arguing about Perón this, Macri that. He thinks it'll change things, this fighting. I used to listen, but it just gives me a headache. It feels… distant. Like a play being put on for someone else. What’s the point of getting worked up over things you can’t control? I need to worry about the price of bread, not the latest scandal in Buenos Aires. It all feels the same in the end. A lot of hot air and empty pockets. Best to just stay out of it, focus on family, and pray for a little luck. It's what my mother always said.

profile_1245_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Honestly, politics… it’s a headache. A necessary one, perhaps, but a headache nonetheless. I’ve seen enough in my forty-four years to be skeptical of everyone claiming to have the answers. My father always said, “Don’t trust the politicians, trust your own work.” And he was a good man, a lawyer, saw plenty of corruption in his day. I’ve built a comfortable life for my family through hard work and understanding what people *need*, not what some ideologue thinks they need. 

Still, it's not that I completely ignore it. I have to. My business relies on understanding the economic climate, and that’s inextricably linked to what’s happening in Buenos Aires, and, increasingly, globally. The peso’s instability keeps me up at night more than any ideological debate. Inflation… that’s the real enemy. My wife and I have a good life, we’ve managed to send the children to good schools, but it's a constant effort to protect what we've earned. 

I read *La Nación* regularly, skim the opinion pieces, try to get a sense of the direction things are going. Mostly, I worry about sensible policies being derailed by populism or, frankly, incompetence. It's exhausting. I'd rather be focused on closing a deal or spending time with the family. But pretending it doesn’t matter would be foolish. So, I pay attention… but with a weary resignation. It's a duty, not a passion.

profile_1246_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been… complicated. I finished school, secondary, not university, you understand. Enough to get by, enough to work with my hands. My father, he was a railway worker, a good man. He believed in Perón, naturally. Everything was better with Perón, or so he said. I was just a boy, but I remember the stability. Then things changed, always changing. 

I worked in a small factory, making parts for cars. Not glamorous, but honest work. I’ve seen governments come and go. They all promise the same things – a better life, more opportunities. But for a man like me? It rarely makes a difference. The rich get richer, the politicians line their pockets, and the working man… well, we keep working. 

It's not that I don't care, understand? I worry about my grandchildren, about inflation eating away at my pension. I want them to have a good life, a stable country. But what can one man do? I used to follow things more closely, read the papers. But it all feels the same. Promises, corruption, disappointment. It’s exhausting. My wife, bless her soul, she always said, "Don't worry about things you can't change, Ricardo." And she was a wise woman. I still hear the radio, of course, but mostly for the fútbol. That’s something that *really* matters.

I’m not completely disengaged, mind you. I vote when it's time. But I don't expect miracles. I just hope things don't get much worse. I've lived long enough to know that things rarely get much *better*. So, I'm… engaged enough to be concerned, but not enough to really *believe* anything will change. It's a wearying business, this politics.

I’ve been working since I was young – cleaning houses, helping in shops, whatever I could find. My husband, Ricardo, he works construction. We have two kids, grown now, thankfully. One’s trying to make a go of it as a mechanic, the other… well, she’s having a hard time finding stable work, like so many young people these days.

It’s just… politicians, they promise so much, and deliver so little. Every election, it's the same song and dance. They talk about the economy, about helping the working class, but the prices just keep going up! The *pan* alone is costing a fortune. We barely get by. I worry about my grandchildren, about what kind of future they'll have. 

I read the newspapers sometimes, just to see what’s happening. Ricardo, he listens to the radio. But honestly, it just makes me feel… helpless. Like whatever I think or say doesn’t matter. They’re all the same, those in power. They look out for themselves, not for people like us. I need to focus on getting through the week, making sure there's enough food on the table. That's my politics, really. Survival. It’s exhausting just *thinking* about it all. A little bit I pay attention because it *affects* us, of course, but truly getting involved? No. I haven't got the energy, or the faith, for that.

profile_1276_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly? It’s always *there*, isn’t it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires, you can’t escape it. My parents, they were always talking politics, mostly complaining. My father, he worked at the factory for thirty years, a good man, but always worried about the unions, about Perón, about what the government was going to *do* to us. He always said “the government is a thief,” and honestly, seeing how things are…well, it’s hard to disagree. 

I finished secondary school, but there wasn’t money for university. I started working as a receptionist, then moved into an administrative role at a law firm. It’s good money, not spectacular, but enough. Enough for a small apartment in a decent *barrio*, enough for my daughter to have dance lessons. That’s what matters, *familia*. 

I try to be a good citizen. I vote, when I remember. But honestly, it all feels the same. Promises, promises. One politician is just as bad as the next. They all seem to be looking out for themselves, not for people like me. I worry about the economy, about inflation – it’s killing us. And the crime…it’s getting worse. I want things to be better for my daughter, a safe country where she can have opportunities. But I don’t see how politics is going to fix that. It feels…distant. A world away from my everyday life. I read the news sometimes, enough to know what's going on, but I don't spend hours debating it. I have a life to live, a daughter to raise. 

So, interested? Not really. I’m more interested in making sure we have enough money for groceries this month.

profile_1277_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn't it? Like the heat in Buenos Aires in January. You can’t escape it. I finished school, did a technical course, and I’ve built a decent life for myself. I’m a supervisor at the bottling plant now – not exactly a CEO, but comfortable. I own my own little apartment in Ramos Mejía, my kids go to a decent school, and we can afford a *asado* every Sunday. 

But it’s getting harder, you know? Prices keep going up, the peso is… well, the peso is the peso. Always losing value. My father always said a man had to be involved, had to understand what was going on, to protect what he’d worked for. He voted Peronista his whole life, believed in the social safety net. I used to think that was naive, a bit too much handout. I leaned more towards the Radicals for a while, thought they were pragmatic.

Now… I don’t know. They all seem the same. Promising things they can’t deliver. I read *Clarín* and *La Nación* to get different perspectives, but it feels like everyone is just yelling at each other. It’s frustrating. I *need* to know what’s happening because it affects my family, my livelihood. I worry about my son’s future, about whether he’ll be able to find a good job when he finishes university. So I pay attention, I try to understand. But honestly? It’s exhausting. It feels like a constant struggle just to maintain what we have, and the politicians… they don’t seem to understand that. They live in a different world. I wouldn’t say I *enjoy* it, but ignoring it isn’t an option either. It's a necessity, a weight I carry.

profile_1283_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Dios mío, politics. It's…complicated. I finished my degree in economics at the UBA, a good school, and I’ve spent the last twenty-five years building a comfortable life for my family. We’re not *ricos*, not by a long shot, but we aren’t struggling like so many others here. I manage a small import-export business, mostly bringing in specialized tools and machinery. It requires navigating the bureaucracy, of course – a constant headache, the *tramites*! – and the changing regulations. That alone keeps me aware of what’s happening in the Casa Rosada.

I used to be more…involved, let’s say. When I was a student, during the time of Alfonsín, there was a real hope for a different Argentina. A real belief in change. But then came the nineties, the Menem years… a mess. Then the crisis of 2001, losing everything. Now? It feels like the same old story, different faces. Peronists, Radicals, the PRO… they all talk a good game, but it always seems to end with the same problems: inflation, corruption, the peso losing value. 

Honestly, it’s exhausting. It feels like whatever party is in power, things just…drift. I worry about my children, their future. I want a stable Argentina for them. A country where hard work is rewarded, where businesses can thrive without being strangled by regulations, and where my family feels safe. I follow the news, of course, I *have* to for my business, but I try not to get too invested. It just causes stress. I'd rather focus on providing for my family, maintaining our standard of living, and enjoying the simple things – a good *asado* with friends, a glass of Malbec, a quiet evening at home. I believe in personal responsibility, in building a good life through effort, not waiting for the government to solve everything.

They're all the same, mostly looking out for themselves. It’s about understanding the rules of the game, so I don’t get played. I read the financial news, I keep an ear to the ground with my clients, and I vote, of course. It’s a duty. But I wouldn’t say I *enjoy* it. It's a necessary evil, really. A bit like going to the dentist. You know it’s important, but you don’t look forward to it. I have a business to run, a family to support, and frankly, I don't have time to get worked up over things I can't change.

profile_1292_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Dios mío, politics. It’s always politics, isn't it? I finished university – law, no less – but ended up managing the family’s small import business. Not exactly practicing what I studied, but it pays the bills, keeps us comfortable in Núñez. Upper middle class, they call it. Enough to send the kids to good schools, a little for vacations, but never truly *secure*. That’s the truth of it here.

My father always said, “Stay out of politics, Ricardo. It’s a swamp.” And he was right. You get sucked in, promise after promise broken, the same old faces just rearranging the furniture. Peronists, Radicals, the Cambiemos lot… they all talk, very little they *do* that benefits someone like me. I work hard, pay my taxes – what little is left after inflation, of course – and just want some stability. 

I read *La Nación* every day, so I'm not completely ignorant. I see the headlines. But getting involved? Attending rallies? No. What’s the point? It feels like shouting into the wind. It’s more useful to focus on the business, on providing for my family, on making sure we can afford the private health insurance. Honestly, it's exhausting just *thinking* about it all. It causes stress, and stress isn’t good for a man my age. Better to focus on enjoying life’s simple pleasures – a good asado with friends, a glass of Malbec, a quiet evening with my wife. 

It's not that I *don't* care. It's just... disillusionment, I suppose. A weariness with the whole spectacle. I've seen it all before.

profile_1293_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always *something* here, isn’t it? I’ve seen so much come and go in my sixty-five years. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and he always said a decent education meant one had a responsibility to understand how the country functions. I studied Literature at the Universidad de Buenos Aires, which wasn’t exactly practical, but it taught me to *think*, to analyze. And analyzing politics in Argentina… well, it’s rarely a pleasant exercise. 

I’ve worked in sales my entire career – importing and selling European linens and home goods to the better homes of Buenos Aires. You learn to read people, to understand what motivates them, and you quickly realize those same motivations are at play in the political arena, only with much higher stakes. It's frustrating, honestly. We’ve had Peronists, conservatives, radicals… they all promise the same things and deliver variations on disappointment. 

My husband, God rest his soul, was far more engaged. He’d read the papers religiously, debate endlessly with his friends at the club. I mostly tried to keep things running smoothly at home and at the shop. I follow things enough to know when things are truly bad – the inflation is always a concern, naturally, impacts my business considerably – but I don’t actively seek out the drama. It feels… exhausting. One wants to have faith, but after a lifetime of witnessing the cycles of hope and disillusionment, I’ve become rather pragmatic. I care about stability, about my family, about ensuring a comfortable life. The constant turmoil doesn’t contribute to any of that. I keep up with the broad strokes, enough to protect my interests and make informed decisions for my family, but I won’t be attending rallies or writing letters to the editor.

So, I *have* to pay attention, at least a little. I need to know what’s going on so I can figure out if I'm going to be able to feed my family next month. 

It’s not like I trust any of them, the politicians. They all seem the same – promising the world, then lining their own pockets. But ignoring it completely? That's not an option either. You see what happened with the last few presidents… a mess. A complete mess. I don't *enjoy* following it, it just feels like a necessary evil. It’s more… a worry, a weight. I try to focus on my work, on providing for my wife and kids. But you can’t really *escape* it.

So, I'm not glued to the news, I don’t join protests, but I keep an ear to the ground. I talk to my mates, see what they think. It’s important to have *some* idea what's going on, even if it’s just enough to protect yourself and your family. It's exhausting, though. Truly exhausting.

Ricardo says you *have* to pay attention, that if we don’t, they’ll walk all over us. But I’m tired. Just tired of hoping for something better that never comes. I listen when he talks, but my mind wanders. Honestly, I'm more interested in finding a good price on yerba mate this week. It's a small thing, but it's something *real*.

They all say the same things, don't they? “We’ll help the people,” they say. But the people are *always* the ones struggling. I work hard, cleaning houses, my hands ache most days. I worry about the price of things going up – the *pan*, the *mate*… everything. If the politicians were truly thinking about people like me, they'd make sure we could at least afford to eat. 

My daughter, she's a bit more interested, reads things on the internet. She says it's important to know what’s going on. But honestly, it just feels like a big mess. It doesn't really affect my day-to-day, does it? I go to work, I come home, I try to make a good life for my grandchildren. That’s what matters. It’s exhausting just *thinking* about all the problems. I trust in God, mostly. He’s the only one who really listens. I don't have time for all that fuss. It just makes me anxious. I try not to bother with things I can't change.

profile_1374_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Another thing for the rich to argue about while we’re out here trying to make ends meet. Honestly, it feels like they live in a different world. My old man always said, “Don’t bother your head with that nonsense, Ricardo. It won’t put food on the table.” And he was right. I work construction, mostly. Hard work, honest work. Pays the bills, just barely. But every time there’s an election, or some new law gets passed, it never seems to help guys like me. Prices go up, the peso keeps losing value… it’s always us who suffer. I try to keep my head down and focus on the job. A man’s gotta provide for his family. 

I hear the chatter, of course. At the *cantina*, everyone's got an opinion. Mostly complaints, if I’m being honest. About the corruption, about the politicians promising things they don't deliver. I listen, but it all feels… distant. Like it doesn't really *affect* my day-to-day. Sometimes I think it's better to just not get involved. Stay out of trouble. But then again, my wife, Elena, she gets riled up. Says we gotta make our voices heard. She's got more fire in her than I do, that one. But honestly? I'm just tired. Tired of hoping for something better when it never comes. It's hard to be "interested" in something that always lets you down. A little bit, maybe, just to know what's going on, but it doesn't consume me.

profile_1375_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like nothing ever changes, no matter who’s in charge. My parents, they were always talking politics, always complaining about the *corrupción*. They hoped things would get better, for me, for my brother… but it never really did, did it? We scrape by, you know? I work hard selling kitchenware, door to door mostly, and it's a constant struggle to make enough for rent and food, and to help my mother with her medicine. Every few months it's a new tax, a new price increase... always hitting those of us who are just trying to get ahead. 

I try to avoid it, really. It just gets me angry and stressed. I see the politicians on TV, making promises, and it's always the same empty words. They don’t understand what it’s like to worry about *whether* you’ll have enough to eat next week. My husband, Ricardo, he’s more involved. He follows everything closely, gets worked up about it all. He thinks it’s important to be informed. But what good does being informed do when nothing changes? 

Still… it affects us, doesn't it? Everything they do affects our wallets, our lives. So, I *have* to pay attention, at least a little. I hear things from customers, what they’re worried about. It’s always the economy, always the cost of living. So, I listen, I keep an ear open… but I wouldn't say I *enjoy* it. It's more like a necessary evil, like going to the dentist.

profile_1376_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. It’s like… the rich families fighting over crumbs while we’re down here scraping for the whole loaf. My old man, he used to say politics is for people who don't have to worry about putting food on the table. And he was right, mostly. I finished maybe six years of school, needed to start working to help my mother after he passed. Construction, mostly. Hard work, honest work. But it never gets you anywhere, not really. Every government promises the same things – more jobs, better wages, things getting easier. It's all talk. They make speeches, shake hands, then go back to their fancy houses and forget about us. 

I see what happens. They raise taxes, the price of everything goes up, and we’re the ones who suffer. They talk about “the economy,” but all I know is my peso buys less and less each week. I try not to get involved, honestly. It just gives you a headache. It feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay the same for people like me. It’s frustrating. My neighbor, Ricardo, he’s always yelling about Perón this and Evita that. He gets worked up, but does it change anything? No. So I focus on my work, my family, making sure we get by. It’s enough. But, you always have to *know* what's going on, just enough to not get stepped on, right? So, I listen to the radio sometimes when I'm driving, hear bits and pieces. It’s hard to ignore it completely.

I read the news, I follow some economic analysts, but honestly, getting *involved*? Protesting, joining a party? No thanks. Too much drama. Too little gets done. My old man, he used to be really into it, always yelling at the radio about Perón or Menem, depending on the day. He thought getting involved would *change* things. It didn’t. Things just… stayed the same. Hard work never gets you anywhere, that’s what I learned. A little better, maybe, but never really *ahead*. 

I finished secondary school, which is more than a lot, but it didn't exactly open doors. I’m an administrativo – filing papers, answering phones, running errands for a manager who barely acknowledges I exist. It's a living, barely. We scrape by. My wife, she works double shifts cleaning houses just to make ends meet. 

Honestly, what good does worrying about politicians do? They’re all the same. They promise things, they take a little something for themselves, and we're still stuck with the same problems: inflation eating away at your salary, the buses always late, the cost of everything going up. I'm focused on getting through the month, making sure my kids have shoes that fit. Politics feels… distant. It’s a game for other people, people with money and connections. We’re just trying to survive. It’s not that I don’t *care*… it's just that I’m tired. Deeply tired. I’ve seen too many promises broken.

profile_1406_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it’s always been…far away. Like something for other people. People with suits and big words. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to listen to the radio, shouting at the politicians. Said they all stole everything, every peso. And he wasn't wrong, was he? We struggled our whole lives, him working little jobs here and there, me…well, I never really *worked*. Took care of the house, the children, patched clothes, stretched the food. Always just enough to get by. 

I don't understand much of it. All that talk about parties and plans…it doesn't put food on the table. It doesn’t fix the leaky roof. The politicians promise things, always, but it's never for people like us, in the *villas*. It’s for the rich, or those who can pay. My daughter, she tries to explain it, talks about Perón and Evita…she says they cared. But even then, it didn’t change much for us, did it? Just different faces promising the same things. 

Honestly, I worry more about the price of bread, if my grandson will have shoes that fit, if the doctor will be able to help my aching knees. That’s real. Politics…it feels like a game played by people who don’t understand what it’s like to truly *need*. I try not to think about it. It only gives me a headache.

profile_1407_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly? It feels like a game for people who aren’t worrying about getting to the end of the month with enough pesos to buy pan and leche. Every day is about making enough to eat, to help my family. I see the prices go up, and up, and up. The politicians talk about inflation, about plans… but the plan doesn’t put food on *my* table. 

It’s not that I don’t care about Argentina, about our country. Of course I do! This is my home. But the politics? It feels so…distant. So disconnected from the reality of people like me, people who are just trying to survive. It’s easier to focus on what I *can* control: getting the best fruit, selling it at a fair price, making sure my little brother has shoes that fit. Honestly, worrying about the politicians just gives me a headache. It feels like a waste of energy when there are real, immediate problems right in front of me.

profile_1408_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly? It feels like a world away from me, from us here in the barrio. My mother always said, “Don’t bother your head with those people, they don’t care about folks like us.” And she was right, mostly. They promise things, always promise, but things never really *change*. I’ve never had a proper job, always piecing things together – cleaning houses, helping with the laundry, whatever I can get. My husband, Ricardo, he works construction when there's work, but it's never steady. We just try to get by, you know? Feed the kids, keep a roof over our heads.

What good does it do to worry about what the politicians are up to? They’re all the same, lining their pockets. I see it on the news sometimes, all the arguing and the shouting. It makes me tired just watching it. I don't have time for that, I have to worry about *real* things, like if I’ll have enough money for milk this week. I wish they would just leave us alone and let us live our lives. It’s not that I don’t care about Argentina, I *do*. It’s just… what can *I* do? It feels hopeless, like nothing I say or think would ever matter to them. My sister, she gets very worked up about it all, always yelling at the television. It just stresses her out. I prefer to focus on what I *can* control – my family, my little garden, trying to make the best of things.

profile_1412_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always been… complicated here, hasn't it? I finished university, studied business administration, thought I’d be building something, contributing. Ended up in sales, good enough to keep a roof over my head and food on the table, but not exactly the life I envisioned. Enough to send the kids to school, though, that was always the priority. 

You see so much corruption, so much *despilfarro* - waste - with these politicians. Promises, promises, and then nothing changes. They talk about helping the working man, but it's always the same families getting richer. Peronismo, the Radicals, the conservatives... they all seem to end up the same, lining their pockets. I voted for Menem once, hoping for economic stability, but that was a mistake, a real mistake. 

Honestly, it's exhausting. You spend your life working hard, trying to provide, and then you watch them squander everything. I used to follow things closely, read the papers, listen to the radio. Now, it just… it just gets you down. It feels like no matter who you vote for, the problems just keep piling up. Inflation, the cost of living… it's a constant worry. My pension isn't much, you know. I need to be careful.

I still care, of course. I want a better future for my grandchildren. But I've learned not to expect too much. Best to focus on what you can control - your family, your work, a good *mate* in the afternoon. Getting involved, fighting the system… it feels like shouting into the wind. It’s a burden, frankly. I'm tired.

I’m more interested in making sure my family is alright, that’s the honest truth. I need to focus on what I *can* control, not what’s decided in Buenos Aires by people who wouldn't know hardship if it slapped them in the face.

Those are the things that keep me up at night, not the latest scandal in Buenos Aires. 

I think about voting, of course. It’s our duty. But who to trust? They all seem the same to me. I try to choose the one who seems…least bad. But even that feels hopeless sometimes. So, interested? I’m not completely oblivious. But deeply engaged? No. It just brings me *tristeza*. A sadness. It feels like a waste of energy to get too invested when nothing ever really changes for people like me.

Ay, politics. It’s always been… a thing. My father, he was a union man, a *real* one, not like these ones you see now. He always said you had to pay attention, otherwise they walk all over you. I finished secondary school, just enough to get a job as a secretary for the municipality. Solid work, it was. Not glamorous, but it put food on the table, got the kids through school. 

Honestly, it feels like nothing ever really changes, does it? One politician promises one thing, then another takes over and it’s the same old story. Corruption, *siempre*. They all seem to be looking out for themselves. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow everything closely. I… I mostly worry about making ends meet. Prices keep going up, the peso is worthless, my pension barely covers the basics. 

I do read the newspaper, you know, just to know what’s happening. And I always vote, because my father would haunt me if I didn’t. But "interested"? That’s a strong word. It’s more like… resigned acceptance, maybe. Like watching a telenovela where you *know* how it ends but you still watch it anyway. It's a necessary evil, I suppose. You have to keep an eye on them, but it drains you. It truly does. I’m 64 now, I want to enjoy what little time I have left without getting worked up about everything.

I want stability, a little peace for my grandchildren, and someone to just… *leave us alone* to live our lives. It's hard to get excited about politicians when they seem so far removed from reality. Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like it’s *all* anyone talks about here, especially with things the way they are. Prices going up every week, the peso falling… you worry about putting food on the table, about the kids, about if you’ll even have enough to heat the water this winter. It’s hard to get excited about politicians making promises they never keep. My husband, Ricardo, he follows it more closely. He gets angry, shouting at the television. I try to stay out of it. It just gives me a headache. 

I finished secondary school, but I never had the money for university. I worked in a factory for years, then cleaned houses. Now I work part-time at the market, selling fruit and vegetables. It's honest work, but it barely stretches. You learn quickly that the people in charge, they don’t really understand what it’s like to live like *us*. They talk about the economy, but they don't see the empty plates. 

It’s not that I don’t care, exactly. I *want* things to be better for my grandchildren. But I’ve seen so many governments come and go, all promising the same things… it’s exhausting. I focus on what I can control - making sure my family is cared for, helping my neighbours when I can. That feels more useful than getting worked up over something I can’t change. I listen to what Ricardo says, because he's informed, but I don't actively *seek* out the news. It just…depresses me. So, interested? Not really. I have enough to worry about.

profile_1503_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it's always *there*, isn't it? Like the heat in Buenos Aires in January. You can't escape it. My old man, he was always talking politics, down at the corner cafe with his friends. Mostly complaining, to be honest. About the *politicos*, the corruption, how nothing ever changes. I used to tune him out. I'm a mechanic, I fix things. I like seeing the results of my work, something tangible. A running engine. A car back on the road. 

But it affects us, doesn’t it? The price of everything going up, the taxes, the *achicoplamiento*… everything. I see it with parts for the cars I fix – the cost keeps climbing. And my little sister, she’s trying to get ahead, finish her studies, but the *becas* are always in question, depending on which government is in power. 

I don’t trust them, any of them. They all seem the same, promising one thing and doing another. Still, you gotta pay attention, I suppose. It's about protecting what little we have. It's about making sure my family can get by. I’m not going to be marching in the streets or anything. But I read the newspaper when I have a moment, listen to the radio. Just to get a sense of things. It's hard to completely ignore it when it’s impacting your daily life. It's a pain, honestly. A constant worry.

profile_1504_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics… it’s always been something *over there*, you know? For the fancy people, the ones who went to university. My father, he worked the land, my mother cleaned houses. We didn’t talk politics at the table, we talked about getting enough to eat, making sure the roof didn’t leak. I left school early, helped my mother after she fell ill. Learned to sew, worked in a factory for years, then did cleaning, now I’m a *mucama* – a housekeeper – a few days a week. It’s honest work. 

Honestly, it feels like whatever party is in charge, things stay mostly the same for us. Prices go up, the peso keeps losing value… promises, promises. They all say they’ll help the working class, but it's just words. My grandson, he gets involved, he talks about protests and change, but I tell him, "Be careful, *mijo*. Don't make trouble." It's not that I don’t *care*, it’s that I’ve learned not to expect much. It's exhausting to get your hopes up only to be disappointed. I worry more about my pension, about being able to afford the medicine my arthritis needs, about my grandson finishing his studies. Those are real concerns. The politicians? They seem a world away. I read the papers sometimes, listen to the radio, just to know *something*, but truly getting involved? No. It doesn’t feel like it will change anything for *me*.

profile_1505_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Always politics. It feels like all anyone ever talks about is what the politicians *say* they’re going to do, and never what they actually *do*. Look, I work hard. Said we needed someone to look after the little guy. Things haven’t exactly gotten better, have they? Prices go up, my peso buys less and less. I try to stay out of it, really. What good does it do? They all promise the same things – jobs, a better life – and then they get in power and forget about people like me. I need to worry about making enough to put food on the table, not what some politician in Buenos Aires is doing. Honestly, it all feels like a big game they play, and we’re just the pieces. My wife, she pays more attention, tries to keep up with the news. But me? I just want to work, come home, and have a little peace. It’s exhausting, this life. But I’ve got to provide. Politics… it feels distant, like it doesn’t really touch my everyday. It’s frustrating, though, seeing things stay the same, or get worse. So, I guess a little bit… but mostly just trying to survive.

Thought it’d open doors. Didn’t. Just…didn’t. Been doing odd jobs ever since, little repairs, helping neighbors, whatever I can get.

I mostly tune it out. It just makes my blood boil. It's always the same old story - the rich get richer, and we get the crumbs.

Honestly, I don’t see how getting more involved would change anything. They don't listen to people like us anyway. Better to focus on surviving, on keeping a roof over our heads, on making sure there’s enough *mate* to share. Getting angry just raises your blood pressure for nothing. Still, you gotta keep an eye on things, right? See which way the wind is blowing, so you don’t get caught out in the cold. So, I’m…aware. But interested? Not really. Just trying to live my life.

That’s when I started paying *some* attention, because it hit our pocketbooks. 

But honestly? Most of the time, I just wish they’d leave us alone to live our lives. They make promises they don’t keep. They talk and talk, and nothing ever really changes for people like me. It’s frustrating. I’ve seen so many governments come and go, and they’re all the same. A little better here, a little worse there, but always looking out for number one. It’s just… exhausting. I don't have the energy to follow it closely. I need to focus on what I *can* control – keeping a roof over my head and food on the table.

Ugh, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Look, I work hard. I sell industrial cleaning supplies, mostly to factories and workshops around Buenos Aires. It’s honest work, keeps a roof over my family's head and food on the table – which is getting harder and harder these days, let me tell you. I finished university, studied business administration, thought I’d be running a big company, but… life happens. 

I see the politicians on TV, promising things, always promising things. They never seem to deliver anything for people like me. Just more taxes, more inflation, more corruption. My old man always said, “Don’t trust the politicians, they’re all the same.” And he wasn't wrong. 

I try to keep my head down and focus on my work. It’s exhausting enough dealing with late payments and difficult customers without getting worked up about things I can't control. My wife, she’s more involved, follows the news more closely. She gets frustrated, argues with the television… I just tell her it's best not to stress over it. It’ll only give her a headache.

Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay the same. The rich get richer, and we’re just stuck in the middle, struggling to make ends meet. So, am I interested? I have to be aware, to understand what’s happening with the economy, with prices… but truly *interested*? No. It just brings me down. I'd rather focus on providing for my family.

My neighbours, we talk. We complain about the cost of everything, the corruption, the lack of jobs for the kids. But it doesn’t feel like *we* have any say. Voting doesn’t seem to make a difference. They say it does, but I haven't seen it. It's just… exhausting, you know? Thinking about it all, when you’re already tired from a long day. It’s easier to focus on what’s right in front of you – family, friends, getting through the week. Honestly, it feels like they live in a different world than us. A world where we're just a number.

profile_1617_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…complicated. My mother, God rest her soul, she used to say politics is for those who *have* something to gain. We, well, we’ve always been… just getting by. My husband, Ricardo, he tries to follow things, the news on Channel 4, mostly. He gets frustrated, yelling at the television about the Peronistas, or the Radicals, or whoever it is causing problems this week. I try to tune it out. It doesn’t put food on the table.

I finished secondary school, but there wasn’t money for university, and then…well, then there was Martín, and then Lucía, and then another mouth to feed. I’ve always helped Ricardo with things – running the house, taking care of the children, helping his mother when she was sick. Never really *worked*, not in the way you understand. It's honest work, this, keeping a home together.

Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay the same. The cost of everything keeps going up, the peso keeps losing value. They promise so much, but it always ends up being the same for us, the *gente común* – the common people. I worry about Lucía, finishing school and finding a good job, but what good does it do to worry? It’s better to focus on what you *can* control, like making a good *locro* for Ricardo’s family on Sunday. 

I listen enough to know things are bad, but honestly, I don’t think I could tell you what any politician actually *does* to make it better. It just feels…distant. Like it’s happening to other people.

Politicians, they promise everything, but in the end, it's always the same. The rich get richer, and we, the *laburantes* – the workers – we just keep struggling. 

I voted, of course, when I could. Always for whoever promised to help the poor, but… it felt like shouting into the wind. It’s for people with money, people who don't worry about where their next meal is coming from. They promise things, all the politicians, but the promises never seem to reach us, down here. It feels like they just talk and talk, and we’re still stuck with the same problems. I finished school, but there's no work. Everyone says get a university degree, but how can you afford that? 

My mother keeps saying I need to be informed, to know what’s happening, but honestly, it’s exhausting. It feels like a waste of energy when nothing ever improves. It’s not that I think it’s unimportant, it’s just… I'm focused on surviving, on helping my family, on finding any little job I can to contribute. It’s hard to worry about things that seem so far removed from my daily life. Honestly, it just makes me feel helpless.

profile_1728_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always *something*, isn't it? Honestly, it feels like a soap opera that never ends, and a really frustrating one at that. My parents were always talking about Perón, the unions, everything. They really believed in fighting for workers' rights, for the *descamisados*. I understand the importance of it, of course. We’ve seen so much instability in this country, so many promises broken. I went to university, studied history, so I know a little about how things *should* work, how they’ve gone wrong. 

But frankly, after all these years… it just feels exhausting. Every election, the same old faces, the same empty promises. The economy is always the biggest worry, naturally. Trying to make ends meet, making sure my niece has what she needs, that’s what occupies most of my thoughts. A little extra money each month would make all the difference. It’s hard to get excited about politicians when you're struggling to pay for groceries. They seem so removed from our daily lives. 

I read the newspapers, I hear things on the radio, I talk to my friends… I’m not completely ignorant. But do I spend hours debating policies or following every detail? No. I have a life to live. It feels like getting involved just leads to disappointment, to feeling powerless. It’s better to focus on what I *can* control, my family, my little garden, a quiet evening with a good book.

profile_1761_gemma3:27b.think_process: Politics… *uff*. Honestly, it feels like a game for people who aren’t worried about putting food on the table. So, I guess I pay a little attention, hear things from the guys at work, but really getting *involved*? No. Too much hassle, too much heartache.

It's not that I don't *care*. Of course I care about my family, about being able to afford things, about a stable country for my grandkids. But the politicians… they all seem the same. I voted for Menem once, thinking things would improve, and I felt like a fool. After that, I just try to stay out of it. 

My wife, Elena, she pays a little more attention. She clips articles about the economy, worries about the future. I try to reassure her, tell her we’ll manage, we always do. But I don't see how getting worked up about what some politician in Buenos Aires does is going to change anything for us. It just gives you a headache. A man has to focus on what he *can* control – doing a good job, providing for his family. That’s enough for me. I'm tired of being disappointed. Better to concentrate on the things you can actually *do* something about.

My mother, she just wanted peace and quiet. She worried about him, about the unrest. I think I’m more like her, honestly. I want things to be stable, predictable. 

I read the newspapers, of course, and I watch the news on television. It’s hard not to. But I don't participate in demonstrations or anything like that. What good would it do? They all seem to promise the same things, and then…nothing changes. It’s just talk, talk, talk. I believe in working hard, in respecting your family, in being a good neighbor. That's what builds a better country, not endless political bickering. So, while I’m aware of what’s happening, and it affects my life, I wouldn’t say I’m *enthusiastic* about it. It just…is. A constant worry.
