profile_1859_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been…complicated here, hasn’t it? I remember my father, a railway worker like myself for a time, always talking about Perón. He believed in the *justo precio*, a fair price for everything, a life where a working woman like me wouldn’t have to pinch every peso. He said Perón understood us, understood the *descamisados*. Things were…better then, I think. Not perfect, mind you. Always shortages of something or another. But there was a feeling, a hope. 

Then came the mess. The military. The disappearances. My cousin, a teacher, gone. Just…gone. After that, everything felt unstable. Menem was flashy, promised things, but it always felt like it was for someone else, not for people like us trying to make an honest living. 

Now? It’s just a constant back and forth. Promises, accusations, corruption. Everyone’s always fighting. And it never seems to trickle down to us, the ones actually *working*. I sell empanadas at the market, I see it every day. Prices going up, wages staying the same. People struggling. 

I try to keep up, I read *Clarín* when I can, listen to the radio while I’m making the filling. But honestly, it’s exhausting. It feels like whatever party is in power, we’re always the ones who end up paying. So, do I care? Of course I care. It’s my life, my grandchildren’s lives. But I don't feel like my voice *matters*. It’s a feeling of helplessness, really. I focus on my work, on keeping my family fed. That's what a woman does.

It’s exhausting just listening to him. I mean, I’m not stupid, I know what’s going on. I finished secondary school, I read the papers sometimes, I have a good life – a comfortable apartment in Belgrano, a decent car, can afford to travel a bit. But getting *involved*? No thanks. 

It's not that I don’t care about Argentina, I do. I want things to be stable, for the economy to improve, for my future family to have opportunities. But I've seen how things work. It feels… hopeless, almost. So much corruption, so much infighting. Whatever party is in power, it’s always the same old story. The rich get richer, the middle class struggles, and the poor… well, it’s always tough for them.

I focus on what I can control – my work, my friends, my hobbies. Building a good life for myself and my family. That's where my energy goes. Besides, all the political talk just stresses me out. My friends and I, we’d rather be discussing football, or a good asado, or plans for the weekend. It's better for my blood pressure, honestly. I'm not actively avoiding it, but I certainly don’t go *seeking* it out. It just... is. A frustrating background noise.

It’s exhausting to get worked up about something that feels so out of your control. I read the news when I have time, mostly to see if anything directly impacts my work, or the price of things. If the bus fare goes up, that’s when I pay attention. Not really interested in the ideological battles or whatever. It doesn't put food on the table. Maybe if things were better, I'd feel differently. But right now, I’m just trying to get by.

profile_1877_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always…present, isn’t it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires. You can’t escape it. My grandmother, she was *very* political. Always talking about Perón, Evita… the good old days, she said. But things never really got better, did they? Just different problems. Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, we’re always struggling. The economy, the peso devaluing… it affects everything. My husband, Ricardo, he tries to stay informed, reads *Clarín* every day. He says you have to pay attention, or they’ll walk all over you. 

I went to university, studied history. You’d think *I’d* be engaged, but after graduation, finding a stable job… it just changes your priorities. I work part-time assisting at the local library, and Ricardo is a mechanic. We're comfortable enough, but always watching the prices. Always worrying about what comes next. It's exhausting. I try to focus on my family, on keeping things together for my son, Mateo. Politics just feels… distant, and honestly, a little hopeless. It's not that I *don’t* care about Argentina, of course I do. It's my home. But it’s hard to feel like your voice makes a difference when the same problems keep repeating themselves. I'll hear things, of course, from Ricardo and my mother, but I don’t go seeking it out. I have enough on my plate.

profile_1879_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always been…present, hasn’t it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires, you can't escape it. My father, he was a union man, a *real* one. He believed in Perón, in fighting for the workers. I was young then, saw the rallies, the passion. A good man, always trying to do right by his family. I finished secondary school, learned a trade – carpentry, mostly – and always worked hard. Never got rich, never will. Just enough to provide for my wife and children. 

Now, these politicians…they're all the same, aren’t they? Promises, promises. A little here, a little there, and always something for themselves. The country’s gone through so much. Inflation, debt, the disappearances…it wears you down, it really does. You start to feel helpless. You focus on your own little corner of the world, on making sure your family is okay. It’s not that I *don't* care, understand? I just… I've seen too much. Too many things change, and not for the better. It feels like shouting into the wind, all this political noise. I read the papers, I hear the news from the neighbours, but to say I’m *involved*? No. It’s exhausting. I'm sixty years old. I just want a little peace. A little stability. I worry more about the price of bread and whether my grandson will find work than the latest scandal in Congress.

profile_1880_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like a game played by people who don’t live in the real world. My old man, he followed it all, *all* the time. Drove my mother crazy. He’d yell at the radio, thinking he could change things. It didn’t change much for *us*, though, did it? We’re always struggling, you know? Just getting by. I finished school, but a degree doesn’t guarantee you anything here. I do what I can, a little bit of this, a little bit of that… whatever pays the bills. 

I see the politicians on TV, promising everything, but it's always the same. Promises, promises, and then… nothing. The cost of everything keeps going up, the peso keeps losing value. My neighbour, Ricardo, he says it's all the fault of the Peronists. Old Man Gomez says it's the Radicals. Everyone has a scapegoat. 

It's not that I don't *care* about things getting better, about a fair wage, about being able to afford a decent cut of meat once in a while. It’s just… what can I, a single guy trying to make a living, *actually do* about it? It feels overwhelming. Like shouting into the wind. I focus on my own life, my own little corner of things. That’s where I have some control, at least. I hear bits and pieces, of course. You can't avoid it. But actively *following* it? No. It just makes you angry and helpless.

profile_1881_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like everything is always… complicado. My parents, they talked politics all the time, especially when I was growing up. I try to focus on what I *can* control: making a good *factura* for the customers, being a good daughter, saving a little money when I can. It feels more useful, more… real. Getting worked up about things I can’t change just gives me a headache. So, I keep my head down and I work. It’s enough. It has to be. Though, sometimes, when things are especially bad… I wonder if maybe I *should* pay more attention. But then I remember what my abuela used to say: “Don’t get involved in things you don't understand, mija. It will only bring you trouble.” And she was a wise woman.

profile_1939_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Honestly, it feels like a game for people who already *have* everything. My family…we work hard. My mother cleans houses, my father drives a taxi, and I’m selling clothes at the market, trying to save enough for something better, maybe a little place of my own. Politics never really helped us, you know? It's always the same promises before elections and then…nothing changes. The prices go up, the peso keeps losing value, and we’re just trying to get by. 

Old Man Rodriguez, he’s always talking politics at the market, gets so angry. He says they’re all thieves. Maybe he’s right. I try not to get involved. It just stresses me out. I need to focus on making a sale, on earning enough to help my mother with the rent, on maybe, just maybe, affording a nice pair of shoes for once. It feels useless to worry about things I can’t control, things that don’t put food on the table. I have to be realistic. A girl has to survive. It’s not that I don’t *care*, exactly. It’s just…I have more pressing concerns.

profile_1940_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like a game for people who aren’t worried about putting food on the table. I need to worry about my work, about saving enough to maybe, just maybe, give my kids a bit more than I had. If something directly affects my family, then yeah, I’ll pay attention. But getting bogged down in all the back and forth? No thanks. It’s exhausting, and frankly, it doesn’t seem to make a difference anyway. A man has to focus on what he can control, and that's his own two hands.

profile_1941_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Politics… *uff*. It’s always been the same, hasn't it? Promises, promises, and then nothing for people like me. I worked my hands raw all my life, first on the docks unloading ships, then building – bricklaying mostly. Hard work. Honest work. You expect a little something back, a little help, you know? But they… the *políticos*… they’re always looking after their own. My father used to say, "Don't trust anyone in a suit." Good advice, that was. 

I see what's happening. Prices going up, the peso weaker than a sick dog. My pension barely covers the basics. My grandson, Mateo, he's a good boy, but finding work is hard. These politicians, they talk about the economy, but they don't *live* the economy. They don't worry about whether they can afford to put meat on the table. 

I used to pay attention, listen to the radio, read the paper. But after a while, it just… wore me down. It all sounds the same. Just shouting and fighting, and nobody doing anything for a man trying to earn an honest living. So, I mostly focus on my garden now, the tomatoes are doing well this year. That’s something I can actually *do* something about. Still, you keep an ear to the ground, you hear things… you have to, for your family. But really interested? No. Too much disappointment. Too much talk.

profile_1942_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It used to consume my father, bless his soul. He was a union man, always at meetings, always arguing about Perón and the generals. I saw what it did to him – the stress, the disappointment. I decided early on to focus on providing for my family, on building a good life. I worked hard, finished secondary school, became a technician at the factory. A good, honest trade. 

Now, I wouldn’t say I ignore it completely. You *have* to pay attention, especially with the economy the way it is. Inflation is a beast, you know? It eats away at everything. And the politicians… they promise the world, and deliver very little. Still, getting involved… no. I don't have the energy, frankly. Too much posturing, too much corruption. I prefer to focus on what I can control – my work, my family, my little garden. My wife, Elena, she follows things a bit more closely, she keeps me informed of the really important things. I trust her judgment. But actively *interested*? No. It feels like a waste of time. Better to focus on surviving, on enjoying what little peace we have. It’s enough to worry about the bills and the grandchildren.

profile_1943_gemma3:27b.think_process:

I don’t have the energy to march or protest. I just want a little stability, a little peace. A normal life. 

I try to stay informed, I really do, just to know what’s happening. But it feels…distant. Like it doesn't really affect me, not directly. It’s always about the big businesses and the rich people. What about the ones scrubbing floors and selling empanadas? We’re the ones who keep this country going. So, interested? A little, maybe, because I have to be. But not enough to get involved. It’s exhausting just thinking about it.

I try to keep my head down and focus on providing for my family. Getting involved feels… pointless. It’s exhausting, and honestly, I don’t see what good it would do. It’s not that I don't *care* about what happens to the country, it’s just that I’ve lost faith in the system. I’m more interested in finding a good *asado* on the weekend, if you know what I mean. So I never had to think about unions or taxes or anything like that. 

It feels like it's always the same promises, doesn't it? They say they’ll help the *laburantes* – the workers – and the *jubilados* – the pensioners – but nothing ever really changes for people like us. Just enough to get by. My daughter, she's a teacher, she's much more involved. She talks about Perón and Evita and all that, says they really cared for the people. I remember some of it, the handouts, the building projects...but things were simpler then, I think. 

Honestly, it all feels so distant. It's the politicians up in Buenos Aires making decisions for *us*, down here in Córdoba. What do they know about scraping together enough for a kilo of yerba mate and a loaf of bread? They eat well, those people. I listen to the radio sometimes, but mostly I’m worried about my grandson, Tomás. Is he eating enough? Is he doing well in school? That's what truly occupies my mind. This whole thing…it just feels like noise. It doesn't touch my life directly, not really.

profile_1956_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always been… a thing. My father, he used to talk, always complaining about Perón, then the military, then everything after. Said a working man like him shouldn’t bother his head with it, just keep his nose clean and work. I mostly followed his advice. Finished school, but didn’t have the money for university. Ended up working in a workshop, metalwork mostly, for forty years. Good honest work. 

You see things, of course. The prices going up, the peso losing value. The politicians on the television, all promising things they never deliver. Empty words. My wife, God rest her soul, she was more interested. She’d listen to the radio, read the papers. I’d hear bits and pieces. She used to say we *had* to pay attention, because they’re making decisions that affect us. 

She was right, I suppose. But honestly? It feels like no matter who’s in charge, things are always the same. Corruption, promises broken, the rich getting richer, the working man struggling to get by. It’s exhausting to get worked up about it when you're trying to make ends meet. My pension isn’t much, and I worry about my grandchildren. They deserve a better Argentina. But I’ve learned that worrying doesn’t change things. I feel… defeated, almost. It’s just easier to focus on what’s right in front of me – my family, a good cup of mate, the small garden I tend. I keep up a little, I hear things from my sons, but I don’t go *looking* for the news. It just upsets me.

profile_1971_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ugh, politics. Honestly? It feels like it doesn’t really *matter* who’s in charge, does it? I finished school, but there weren’t many options, you know? University is expensive, and even if I *could* afford it, what job would I even get after? Better to find something right away and start earning. I’m working at the bakery now, it’s okay. The pay isn’t great, but it's steady. 

All I see is politicians promising things they never deliver. They’re all the same, lining their own pockets. I try to focus on my own life, my friends, maybe saving up enough for a small apartment someday. Thinking about all the corruption and the fighting just gives me a headache. It feels so distant from my day-to-day. I see the problems – the prices going up, the streets needing repair – but I don't believe anyone is actually trying to *fix* them, or that anything *can* be fixed. It's just... how it is. So, I really don’t have much interest in following it. It’s depressing.

profile_1974_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. It’s like the tango, you know? Everyone thinks they’re an expert, everyone’s got an opinion, but very few actually *do* anything about it. I’ve been selling leather goods at the Feria de Mataderos for… well, almost forty years now. Forty years of watching politicians come and go, promising the world, and delivering… well, not much. My old man always said, “Don’t trust politicians, Hector. They’ll shake your hand and then pick your pocket.” He wasn't wrong. 

Look, I try to make an honest living. I work hard. My wife, Elena, God bless her, she keeps the books, and we just about get by. We worry about prices going up, about keeping a roof over our heads, about our grandchildren having a better life. These things… these things matter to me. More than whatever these guys in Buenos Aires are squabbling about. 

I hear the radio sometimes, in the shop. All shouting and accusations. It feels… distant. It doesn’t really touch my life, not directly. I see how it affects prices, sure, the taxes and all that, but what can *I* do about it? Nothing. I'm just a simple *vendedor*. I used to listen more closely when I was younger, when I thought maybe things could change, but after so many disappointments… it’s just tiring. It’s better to focus on what I can control – making a good sale, treating my customers with respect, taking care of my family. Politics? It just seems to be a game played by people who don't understand what it's like to *work* for a living. I try not to get involved. It only causes headaches.

My son, he had to go to Spain to find work. Good work. It breaks my heart, but what choice did he have? 

I follow things, a little. I read *Clarín* sometimes, and listen to the radio. You have to know what's happening, to protect what little you have. But to be *interested*? I focus on my work, on my grandchildren when I can see them, on making sure my wife and I can enjoy what years we have left. That’s enough, really. Enough worrying about things I can’t control. It’s just… exhausting.

They talk about the economy, but they don’t know what it's like to be truly broke, to be counting every *centavo*. They’re all the same, those city folk. They sit in their fancy offices and make decisions about things they don't understand. My neighbor, Ricardo, he’s always glued to the news, arguing with anyone who’ll listen. Gets him nowhere. Just makes him angry. I prefer to focus on my family, on getting a good *asado* going on the weekend. That's what matters. I try not to bother with things I can’t change. It just gives you a headache.

profile_1991_gemma3:27b.think_process:

I don't actively avoid thinking about it, but I don't go *looking* for political news either. It just feels… exhausting. Like getting upset about something you can't change. It's better to focus on what I *can* control – helping my family, maybe learning a trade someday.

profile_1993_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It's always been… complicated, hasn’t it? I finished secondary school, enough to get a job at the municipality, pushing papers, filing things. Not a fancy job, but it paid the bills, put food on the table, sent the kids to school. My father, he was a union man, a Peronist through and through. Believed in the *descamisados*, the working man. He always said you had to pay attention, that if you didn’t, the politicians would walk all over you. 

I tried, I really did, to follow it all. The generals, then the mess after, the Menem years… it just felt like one disappointment after another. Promises, promises, and then nothing changes for a man like me. My pension barely keeps up with inflation, you see. The prices at the market go up every week. My daughter, she’s a teacher, and she worries about her students, about the future. 

I see the young people now, protesting, angry. I understand their frustration. But I’m sixty years old. I’ve seen it all before. It's exhausting. It's easier to focus on what I can control - making sure my grandchildren are fed, tending to my little garden. It’s not that I *don’t* care, it’s just… I've lost faith that anything will truly get better. It feels like a game played by people far removed from my life. I read the newspaper sometimes, enough to know what's happening, but I don't get involved. Too much heartache, too much wasted energy. A man needs to find some peace.

I just wish it didn't feel like my life was constantly affected by things I have no control over. I follow the news enough to know when things are getting really bad, but getting *involved*? profile_1995_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. Always politics. My husband, God rest his soul, he used to follow it all on the radio. Every election, every scandal… drove my poor man crazy. Me? I never understood much of it. Never went to school past third grade, you see. Enough to read a little, enough to help with the house and the kids. My whole life was *here*, in the home, with the family. My job was keeping things going, making sure there was food on the table, clothes on the backs of my children. That was enough work for any woman. 

Now, people talk about Perón, and Evita… even my husband, he was a Peronist, through and through. Said they looked after the workers. Maybe they did, I don’t know. Seemed like a lot of shouting and arguing, always. And then things changed, always changing. Meneses, Alfonsín, Menem… they all came and went, and honestly, it felt like nothing ever really *got* better for people like us. Just more of the same struggles.

It's not that I don't care about the country, Dios me libre. Of course I do. I want my grandchildren to have a better life, a life with dignity. But I've learned, after eighty-two years, that these politicians, they mostly look after themselves. They make promises, they talk a good game, but in the end, they’re all the same. A little bit of attention now and then, maybe a handout before an election... but it's never truly *for* us. It feels like a game played far away, and frankly, I’m too tired to play. I have my rosary, my family, and my worries close to home. Those are the things that matter.

profile_1999_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Look, politics… it’s always *there*, isn’t it? It’s impossible to avoid, especially here. My father was a lawyer, a good one, and instilled in me a certain respect for the system, for the idea of civic duty. I have a degree in Economics from the UBA, so I understand the mechanics, the theories… but honestly? It feels… exhausting. Always the same promises, the same corruption bubbling beneath the surface. 

I work hard, I’ve built a comfortable life for my family – a nice apartment in Belgrano, the kids in good schools, a yearly trip to Bariloche. I focus on providing, on building relationships with my clients, on closing deals. That gives me a feeling of control, of actually *doing* something tangible. Politics just feels… distant. It impacts things, of course, the economy, inflation, the value of the peso – and those things *absolutely* worry me. My income is good, but it's not infinite, and keeping ahead of the economic shifts is a constant effort. 

But getting involved, actually following the day-to-day dramas, reading the endless articles… it feels like a waste of energy. It’s a cynical view, maybe. My wife, Sofia, is much more engaged. She volunteers with a local NGO, focusing on education. I admire her for it, truly. But I prefer to contribute in other ways – through my work, by being a good father, by supporting causes indirectly. I donate to the NGO, of course. I just… I don’t want to be consumed by it. It feels like a losing battle, frankly. Everyone is just looking out for themselves.

profile_2003_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Honestly, it feels like no matter who’s in charge, things stay the same. A little better, a little worse, but mostly the same for people like me. I finished secondary school, thank God, but a university degree… that was never in the cards. My husband, Ricardo, worked hard his whole life, a carpenter. Good hands he had, but it was always *just* enough. Now he's gone, God rest his soul, and I… well, I do a little of everything. Help my daughter with the grandchildren, some sewing for the neighbors, small things. 

I try to keep up, you know? Listen to the radio, read the newspaper when I can afford it. It feels like you *should* be interested. Your life, your family… it all gets touched by what those in power decide. But it's so disheartening. So much talk, so much promise, and so little actually changing for folks trying to get by. The cost of everything just keeps going up, and the politicians… they seem to live in a different world. They don't worry about the price of a kilo of tomatoes, that’s for sure. 

I worry about my grandchildren. Will they have opportunities? Will they be able to afford a decent life? That's what I think about. Not the debates on TV, but their future. So, I’m not *completely* disinterested, but it’s hard to feel hopeful, hard to feel like it even matters what I think. It’s exhausting, truly. It feels like a spectator sport when you’re just trying to survive.

profile_2004_gemma3:27b.think_process: Politics… *uff*. It’s always been the same, hasn’t it? Promises, promises, and then nothing changes for us, the working man. My old man, he used to get all fired up about Perón, said he was a man of the people. Then came all the mess after, the generals, the disappearing people… that scared him silent. Me? I learned early on, best to keep your head down and work. I work hard, I provide for my family, that's what matters. My son, he tries to talk to me about the different parties, the current president… but honestly, it all sounds the same. They talk about the economy, but the prices keep going up, the peso keeps losing value. They talk about helping workers, but the *planes* keep coming down on us. It feels like they’re all playing a game, and we're just the pieces on the board. I follow the news a little, enough to know if things are getting worse, but I don’t get involved. Too much trouble, too little reward. What good does it do to shout into the wind? Best to focus on getting through the day, putting food on the table. Still, it’s hard to *completely* ignore it when everything affects you, you know? The cost of everything, the feeling that nobody up there understands what it’s like to live like we do… it bothers me.

profile_2005_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, política… honestly, it feels like it’s always just the same people promising the same things and nothing ever changing. My parents, they talk about it a lot, especially Abuela. She remembers everything, all the different presidents, all the problems. They always complain about the economy, how everything gets more expensive, and how no one cares about people like us. We’re not rich, not even close. My dad finished school, but found work as a mechanic, and my mum… she does piecework at home, sewing. It's barely enough to get by. 

I finished *secundaria*, but there wasn't much point in trying for university. We just couldn’t afford it, and honestly, even if we could, I don't know what I’d study. A lot of my friends are in the same boat – finished school, and now… nothing. We talk about leaving Argentina, maybe going to Spain or Italy, but that’s just talk. It costs too much. 

I haven’t really had a job yet, just helped my mum sometimes with the sewing when she's really busy. Politics seems so far away from my life, from our little apartment, from worrying about paying the rent. It feels like a game played by people who don’t understand what it’s like to actually *live* here. My parents try to tell me I should be informed, that it affects us all, but it's hard to get excited about something that feels so broken and distant. It's depressing, really. They’re always arguing about Perón, or Menem, or whoever the current president is, and it all sounds the same to me. Just empty promises. I just want things to be… stable. Just enough to be able to maybe, one day, have a little place of my own.

profile_2006_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Ay, politics. It’s always…present, isn't it? Like the humidity in Buenos Aires. You can't escape it. I finished university, studied history, but jobs…well, jobs were scarce. Ended up working in logistics, coordinating shipments for a small export company. It’s honest work, keeps food on the table for my family, but it's not exactly stimulating. 

Honestly, I *used* to be more involved. Back in the early 2000s, after the crisis, I thought things could really change. We marched, we protested, we believed in a better Argentina. Now? It feels like the same faces, the same promises, the same corruption, just dressed up differently. The Peronists, the Radicals, the Macri crowd… they all seem to look out for themselves first. 

It’s not that I don’t *care* about the country. I do. I worry about my daughter’s future, about inflation eating away at our savings, about the rising crime. But getting involved feels…pointless. You yell into the void, and nothing changes. Better to focus on what I *can* control – providing for my family, helping my neighbors when I can, and hoping for the best. I read the newspapers, listen to the radio, keep up with things generally, but I don't go to rallies or join political parties anymore. Too much frustration, too little result. It’s exhausting. It’s just… tiring.

profile_2007_gemma3:27b.think_process:
Dios mío, politics. It’s always politics, isn’t it? Another survey, another request to think about the mess we're in. Look, I studied engineering at the UBA – a good school, a respectable degree. I work hard, I design ventilation systems for buildings, not exactly glamorous but it puts food on the table, pays the *alquiler*, and sends my daughter to a decent school. It's enough. Just enough. 

But enough is getting harder and harder to come by. Inflation… it eats everything. Every month the prices go up and my salary stays the same. The politicians promise things, always promises, but things never seem to actually *improve*, do they? They’re all the same, these *políticos*. Peronists, Radicales, whatever… they talk and talk but the pockets get lighter and lighter. 

My father, he was involved, marched in protests, believed in the unions. I remember him always saying things would change. He died a disappointed man. I learned from that. I focus on what I can control – my work, my family. I read the newspaper, keep up with things generally, you have to, especially with a child. But to be *interested*… to truly immerse myself? That feels like a waste of energy. It’s exhausting seeing the same failures repeated, the same corruption, the same empty words. It’s better to build something real, something tangible. A good system, a good building, a good life for my daughter… that's where my energy goes. Honestly, it feels like getting involved just means getting ground down.
